title: Why bedtime is the most triggering part of the day
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The silence is where the noise lives

I used to dread bedtime. Not because I wasn't tired. I was exhausted. Bone-deep, soul-crushing tired. But the moment my head hit the pillow, my brain would light up like a pinball machine. Every fear, every failure, every moment of shame from the day would come rushing in. And I'd lie there, heart pounding, staring at the ceiling, wondering why sleep felt like a battlefield instead of a relief.

Here's the thing. I'm not alone in this. Bedtime is the most triggering part of the day for so many of us with trauma histories. And it's not random. It's not a personal failing. It's biology. It's the nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do.

Think about it. During the day, you're busy. You're working, parenting, scrolling, running errands, keeping yourself distracted. Your brain is occupied. There's noise. There's motion. There's something to focus on besides the raw, unprocessed sensations living inside your body.

But at night, the distractions fall away. The lights go dim. The house gets quiet. And suddenly, you're alone with yourself. That's when the nervous system says, "Oh, now we have time to deal with all the stuff we've been avoiding."

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, explained this in terms of neuroception. That's the subconscious system your body uses to scan for safety and danger. During the day, your neuroception might pick up enough cues of safety from your environment to keep you regulated. But at night, those cues disappear. The dark feels vulnerable. The quiet feels threatening. Your body doesn't know the difference between a safe bedroom and a prehistoric cave where predators might lurk. So it defaults to survival mode.

Your heart rate goes up. Your breathing gets shallow. Your mind starts racing with worst-case scenarios. You're not broken. You're just wired for survival in a world that doesn't feel safe.

And that's the core of it. Bedtime triggers the parts of us that never got to rest.

Your body remembers what your mind wants to forget

I remember reading Bessel van der Kolk's work years ago, specifically his book "The Body Keeps the Score." He talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just in the mind. And bedtime is when the body speaks the loudest.

Here's why. When you lie down, your body shifts into a horizontal position. That position is evolutionarily linked to vulnerability. Think about it. Animals sleep lying down. They're exposed. They're defenseless. Your body knows this on a cellular level. So when you lie down, especially if you have a trauma history, your body might interpret that as a signal of danger. It might say, "We can't let our guard down. Something bad is going to happen."

Wild, right? But it gets deeper.

Many trauma survivors have a fragmented relationship with their own bodies. Janina Fisher, a leading expert on structural dissociation, describes how trauma splits the self into parts. You might have a part that's hypervigilant, scanning for threats. Another part that's numb, checking out when things get too intense. Another part that's angry, protective, ready to fight. And when you lie down in the dark, all those parts start competing for your attention.

The hypervigilant part says, "Stay awake. Stay alert. Don't let them get you."

The numb part says, "I'm already gone. I can't feel anything. What's the point?"

The angry part says, "Why should I rest when the world never let me rest?"

You're not just one person trying to fall asleep. You're a committee of traumatized parts trying to negotiate a ceasefire. And that's exhausting.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It's not light reading, but it explains this dynamic in a way that made me feel seen.

The shame spiral that hijacks your sleep

There's another layer to this. Bedtime isn't just about fear. It's about shame.

How many times have you lain in bed replaying a conversation from earlier that day? Rehashing something you said or didn't say. Cringing at yourself. Feeling that hot wave of embarrassment wash over you at 11 PM when you should be drifting off.

I've done it more times than I can count. And I used to think it was just anxiety. But it's not. It's shame. And shame is a trauma response.

Pete Walker, who wrote extensively about complex PTSD, described this as the "inner critic." That voice inside your head that tells you you're not good enough, you're failing, you're a mess. And that voice gets louder at night because there's nothing to drown it out.

During the day, you can distract yourself. You can work, exercise, scroll social media, talk to people. But at night, the inner critic has the floor. And it uses that time to run through every mistake you've ever made, every way you've disappointed yourself or others, every reason why you should feel bad about who you are.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why that inner critic sounds so much like the people who raised me.

The shame spiral is brutal because it's self-reinforcing. The more you lie there feeling bad, the more your nervous system gets activated. The more activated you are, the harder it is to sleep. The harder it is to sleep, the more exhausted you are the next day. And exhaustion makes you more vulnerable to shame the following night. It's a loop. A vicious, exhausting loop.

But here's what I've learned. That shame spiral isn't a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your nervous system is trying to protect you. It's saying, "If I can just figure out what I did wrong, maybe I can prevent it from happening again. Maybe I can keep us safe." It's a misguided attempt at control. But it's not helpful. And it doesn't have to run your life.

What actually helps your nervous system at bedtime

I'm not going to tell you to do a 10-step bedtime routine with lavender oil and gratitude journals. That's wellness industry nonsense that assumes your nervous system just needs some nice smells to calm down. It doesn't work that way.

What does work is understanding that your nervous system needs cues of safety, not just relaxation. There's a difference.

Relaxation is something you do. Safety is something your body perceives. You can't force your body to feel safe. You can only create conditions where safety might emerge.

Here are a few things that actually helped me. Not as a checklist, but as experiments. Try one. See what happens. Don't force it.

First, temperature. Your body needs to cool down to fall asleep. That's biology. But for trauma survivors, being cold can feel threatening. So find a middle ground. A warm bath or shower before bed can help your body temperature drop afterward. It's a signal to your nervous system that it's time to rest.

Second, pressure. Deep pressure stimulation is calming for many people. It activates the parasympathetic nervous system, the part that says "rest and digest" instead of "fight or flight." A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). I use one myself. It feels like being held, which is something many of us never got enough of.

Third, sound. Silence can be terrifying for a traumatized nervous system. It's too open, too vulnerable. White noise, pink noise, brown noise, or even just a fan can create a sonic buffer. It fills the space so your brain doesn't have to. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They don't block everything, but they take the edge off.

Fourth, movement. I know, it sounds counterintuitive. But sometimes your body needs to release energy before it can rest. Gentle shaking, stretching, or even just rocking back and forth can help discharge the stored tension from the day. Peter Levine talks about this in his work on somatic experiencing. Your body holds trauma in its tissues. Sometimes you need to move to let it go.

And fifth, permission. This is the hardest one. Give yourself permission to not sleep. I know that sounds backwards. But the pressure to fall asleep is itself a trigger. The more you try to force sleep, the more your nervous system resists. So lie there. Rest your body. Let your mind wander. If you don't sleep, you don't sleep. That's okay. Your body is still getting some benefit from the rest. And taking the pressure off can actually help you fall asleep.

What bedtime really asks of you

I'm not going to pretend I have bedtime figured out. I still have nights where I lie awake, heart pounding, mind racing, feeling like a scared child in an adult body. Those nights are hard. And they're real.

But I've stopped fighting them. I've stopped telling myself I'm broken for having them. I've stopped believing that a good night's sleep is something I earn by being a good enough person.

Bedtime is triggering because it asks you to be still. To be quiet. To be present with yourself. And for those of us who learned that being present with ourselves was dangerous, that's a terrifying request.

Your nervous system learned to stay alert at night for a reason. Maybe you weren't safe. Maybe the dark meant something bad was coming. Maybe you had to stay vigilant to survive. That was real. And your body still remembers.

But here's what I want you to know. You're not in that situation anymore. Even if it feels like you are. Even if your body screams at you to stay awake. You're in a different time and place. And slowly, over time, your body can learn that too.

Not by force. Not by willpower. But by repeated, gentle experiences of safety. By lying down and noticing that nothing bad happens. By letting your breath slow down just a little. By giving yourself the compassion you never got.

Bedtime might always be hard. That's okay. You don't have to fix it. You just have to show up for yourself, night after night, with patience and kindness and a willingness to be exactly where you are.

Sit with that.
